channel_name: This Week in Startups · Jason Calacanis
video_title: The AI Tutor That Makes Kids Actually Think | E2298
gist: Brilliant CEO Sue Kim explains how the company launched an AI Socratic tutor ('Cooji') built on seven years of proprietary interactive-lesson infrastructure, pricing it against $10K/year human tutors rather than apps, and argues vertical tutoring data beats frontier models that have plateaued on teaching since GPT-o1. Calacanis closes with a treatise on standard deal terms and 'VCs behaving badly' war stories.

insights.claim: Brilliant's AI tutor 'Cooji' is largely model-agnostic; its differentiator versus frontier models is a verifiable reward signal for tutoring (whether the student actually understands) that the company has accumulated at scale.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Suggests the durable moat in EdTech AI is proprietary learning-loop data, not the underlying LLM, which is commoditizing.
insights.quote: frontier models don't have verifiable reward signals for tutoring and we do

insights.claim: Frontier models' ability to tutor well has not improved much since OpenAI's o1, because the core job of diagnosing and fixing student misunderstandings has plateaued absent a real verifiable reward source for reinforcement learning.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Implies a defensible gap for verticalized AI tutors that own the reward signal; raw model scaling won't close it soon.
insights.quote: their ability to tutor well hasn't improved much since like 01

insights.claim: Brilliant has built interactive-canvas infrastructure since 2019 (GPT-2 era) where every canvas exposes an API the LLM can read and write to, letting the tutor see, draw on, and annotate the student's work graphically.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Seven years of pre-built infrastructure is the entry barrier for competitors trying to bolt AI onto a learning product.
insights.quote: since 2019 literally since GPT2 we have been designing an interactive uh canvas infrastructure where every interactive canvas has an API that LLM can read and write to

insights.claim: Brilliant constrains LLMs to a narrow role; pedagogy and mathematical correctness live in a more deterministic lesson-infrastructure layer, while teaching every concept required expert teachers to manually instruct the model on tool calls and common misconceptions.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Reliable AI tutoring requires heavy human-expert curation, not plug-and-play LLMs; signals a labor-intensive build others must replicate.
insights.quote: there there's very little of this that we get for free from LLM

insights.claim: Brilliant gets language localization 'for free' from frontier models, with tutor voices (some from employees) sounding uncanny in perfect Korean, enabling expansion into any language.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Model advances directly lower Brilliant's cost to enter international markets, where 60% of users already are.
insights.quote: The localization is astonishingly good

insights.claim: Brilliant believes a self-improving, hyper-personalizing tutor built on a dense user model and learning graph will not come out of a model company; it must be vertically purpose-built for the tutoring use case.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Counters the view that OpenAI/Google will absorb EdTech; argues for standalone vertical-AI investments.
insights.quote: we don't believe that that's going to come out of a model company anytime soon. like you have to vertically purpose build it

insights.claim: Cooji's UX fades visual scaffolding as the student approaches mastery, ending in a test-like environment where the tutor disappears and the student works unaided.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Pedagogical design choice that differentiates 'AI that makes you think' from AI that does the work for you, the core brand wedge.

themes.theme: Consumer vs B2B and premium pricing strategy

insights.claim: Brilliant chose direct-to-consumer over selling to schools because B2B puts multiple steps (district, teacher, student) between the company and the learner, removing the real-time feedback loop and licensing a product kids are forced to use rather than want to use.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Argues consumer EdTech yields better product iteration; a lens for evaluating EdTech business models.
insights.quote: one of the problems with selling to schools is that you're pretty far from the actual learner

insights.claim: Brilliant prices at roughly $20-$40/month, benchmarking against a human tutor (~$10,000/year) rather than against games or typical apps that go for $60-$100/year, aiming to deliver most tutor capabilities at a ~95% cost reduction.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Anchoring price to the displaced service (tutors), not the app category, supports premium consumer pricing power.
insights.quote: we wanted to bring that cost down by 95%. Like we wanted to be able to offer a product for 30 bucks a month that could do most of the things that you would hire a tutor

insights.claim: Brilliant reports that customers' main pricing question is 'why is this so cheap' rather than 'why is this so expensive,' indicating the tutor anchor gives meaningful pricing headroom.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Signals untapped pricing power and that consumers accept the tutor-replacement value framing.
insights.quote: the main pricing question from our customers is not why is this so expensive, it's why is this so cheap

insights.claim: App store reviews, refund requests, trial behavior, and granular parent emails form a real-time product feedback loop Brilliant treats as 'gold,' with multiple humans reading every single review.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Reinforces consumer-direct as a product-development advantage unavailable to B2B EdTech.
insights.quote: the app store reviews are gold

insights.claim: High-end private tutoring runs $150-$300 per 90-minute session in Silicon Valley/Austin and about $80/hour in middle America, but effectiveness requires high-dosage tutoring (~3x/week) that most families cannot afford.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis / Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Quantifies the affordability gap Brilliant targets and the willingness-to-pay ceiling for human tutors.
insights.quote: I think it's $150 to $300 per like 90 minute session

themes.theme: AI sentiment and the 'makes you think' wedge

insights.claim: Brilliant's tutor launch hit ~5 million views on X despite AI being 'immensely uncool and unpopular with consumers,' suggesting 'AI that makes you think' is a far more popular positioning than AI that does the thinking for you.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Marketing/positioning signal: pro-human, augmentation-framed AI products may face less consumer backlash.
insights.quote: AI that makes you think is an incredibly popular idea

insights.claim: Consumers are not anti-AI broadly but anti-idiocracy, anti-IP-theft, anti-job-replacement and anti-slop; products that visibly help users overcome this resistance.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Reframes 'AI backlash' as targeted, giving a template for which AI products win consumer goodwill.
insights.quote: people aren't anti-AII... They're anti-IP theft. They're anti- being replacing their jobs. They're anti-slop.

insights.claim: Calacanis argues recent graduation-season booing of AI stems from the first cohort to use AI in school resenting a 'university slop into job slop' future where their work felt unnecessary.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Identifies a generational sentiment risk that products framing students as empowered (not replaced) can exploit.
insights.quote: their entire college experience was university slop and they're going to go into a world where their job will be job slop

insights.claim: Sue Kim frames AI as a forcing function dividing users into better or worse thinkers, betting that 'AI used well can make kids into geniuses.'
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Captures the mission-driven demand thesis underpinning Brilliant's consumer pull.
insights.quote: AI clearly is going to either make you smarter or it's going to make you passive and dumb

insights.claim: Brilliant teaches transferable problem-solving (seeing the structure of problems) rather than school-style procedure memorization, which it considers a brittle approach that breaks when a learned procedure doesn't apply.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Differentiates the product's value proposition and explains parent willingness to pay tutor-level prices.
insights.quote: the difference is knowledge versus problem solving. And what brilliant teaches is problem solving.

insights.claim: Training in math or coding builds a 'systems brain' that gives intuition for what AI problems can and can't be solved, making problem-solving skill increasingly valuable in an AI world.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Positions STEM-style learning as the durable human skill complementary to AI, supporting long-term demand.
insights.quote: that's what learning math or coding trains... you get really good at seeing how to apply AI to your life and work

insights.claim: Parents overwhelmingly still believe learning hard things matters and fear their kids aren't learning to read, write, or do arithmetic at school, a fear amplified by data on declining literacy and numeracy.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Identifies the demand driver Brilliant taps; declining school outcomes expand the addressable market for at-home tutoring.
insights.quote: American parents see the data that our kids can't read or do math anymore

insights.claim: Brilliant has about 70 employees, with roughly 40% of users in the US and 60% international, and does not publicly disclose revenue or membership numbers.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Lean team relative to scale; large international base implies localization is a real growth lever.
insights.quote: about 40% of our users in the US, but 60% are all around the world

insights.claim: Brilliant's roadmap is a tutor in every home, language, and subject: near curriculum-complete in middle/high-school math, tackling college-level next, completing foundational coding this year, and expanding into science next year.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Once the tutor framework exists, new verticals could ship in months rather than years, accelerating revenue expansion.
insights.quote: Our vision is a tutor in every home, in every language, in every subject.

insights.claim: Sue Kim calls the moment the 'ChatGPT moment for AI and learning,' predicting that fine-tuning models specifically for tutoring is near and will transform how everyone learns.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Signals an inflection in EdTech AI worth watching for entry timing.
insights.quote: this is like the chat GBT moment for AI and learning

insights.claim: Brilliant sees deep recognition that its unique scaled tutoring dataset could fine-tune models to be very good at tutoring; Calacanis notes portfolio company Micro One does training/learning work for language-model companies.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim / Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Brilliant's data could become a separate B2B revenue line or strategic asset for model labs.
insights.quote: we have tutoring sessions at scale with real students

insights.claim: Brilliant originated when Chamath Palihapitiya argued the prior company All Tuition (a LendingTree/Bankrate for private student loans, processing hundreds of millions in consolidations) would end up incentivized to keep student debt complex rather than fix it.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Illustrates founder pivot logic; the incentive-alignment critique applies to fintechs that profit from a problem persisting.
insights.quote: your incentives are to lobby the government to keep student loans really complicated and expensive so that you can make money saving consumers

insights.claim: Calacanis frames AI as 'jet fuel' for established companies that understand their customers, urging founders to add boosters and disrupt themselves rather than fear disruption.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Investment lens favoring established consumer companies with real data/customer relationships as AI beneficiaries.
insights.quote: AI is jet fuel. Well, you know, for your rocket ship.

insights.claim: Calacanis argues inexperienced VCs wrongly reject companies that don't fit an existing TAM, citing Airbnb, ride-sharing, and eBay as having near-zero initial TAM because they induced markets to exist.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Investor heuristic: category-creating startups are systematically underpriced by TAM-bound VCs.
insights.quote: The TAM for Airbnb was basically zero. They induced a market to exist.

themes.theme: VC deal terms, conduct & founder process

insights.claim: Calacanis advocates standard deal documents and flags non-traditional structures, advising founders to avoid high-net-worth individuals demanding board control: a board observer seat for ~5% ownership and a full board seat above 10%.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Concrete governance benchmarks founders can use to spot predatory or off-market deal terms.
insights.quote: If somebody owns 5%, you can give them a board observer seat. If they over 10%, give them a board seat.

insights.claim: Calacanis's firm runs over 100 first meetings, automatically solicits founder feedback after each, scores it, and restricts first calls to only the three highest-scoring team members ('three-point shooters').
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Operational playbook for running a high-throughput, founder-respectful VC funnel; a benchmark for fund quality.
insights.quote: first calls are only done by the three highest scored people

insights.claim: Calacanis ends every founder meeting by asking 'May I repeat your vision back to you' to signal attention, surface omissions, and correct misunderstandings, a tactic he made mandatory across his team.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Repeatable diligence technique that improved his firm's founder-feedback scores; a signal of investor quality.
insights.quote: May I repeat your vision back to you so that I can make sure I understand it perfectly.

insights.claim: Calacanis claims VCs who spend more than a couple of years in venture absorb 'bad habits' (one meeting a day, entitlement from check-writing power), prompting him to run an associate-in-training program instead of hiring experienced VCs.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Hiring/culture signal; contrarian view that VC experience can be a negative for a high-velocity fund.
insights.quote: they think that their power dynamic is that because they write a check... they get to dictate these things. They don't.

insights.claim: Calacanis frames VC as a high-trust system where investors hand founders millions on faith and founders trust investors not to block future sales or rounds, which is why standardized terms matter.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Explains why off-market terms break the system and should be a red flag for both sides.
insights.quote: it's a trustbased system
